[![Showcase](/docs/res/showcase-thumb.png)](https://youtu.be/lazLNcEYsiQ) ## A personal, organic agentic framework that grows and learns with you - Agent Zero is not a predefined agentic framework. It is designed to be dynamic, organically growing, and learning as you use it. - Agent Zero is fully transparent, readable, comprehensible, customizable, and interactive. - Agent Zero uses the computer as a tool to accomplish its (your) tasks. # ⚙️ Installation Click to open a video to learn how to install Agent Zero: [![Easy Installation guide](/docs/res/easy_ins_vid.png)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=w5v5Kjx51hs) A detailed setup guide for Windows, macOS, and Linux with a video can be found in the Agent Zero Documentation at [this page](./docs/setup/installation.md). ### ⚡ Quick Start ```bash # Pull and run with Docker docker pull agent0ai/agent-zero docker run -p 50001:80 agent0ai/agent-zero # Visit http://localhost:50001 to start ``` # 💡 Key Features 1. **General-purpose Assistant** - Agent Zero is not pre-programmed for specific tasks (but can be). It is meant to be a general-purpose personal assistant. Give it a task, and it will gather information, execute commands and code, cooperate with other agent instances, and do its best to accomplish it. - It has a persistent memory, allowing it to memorize previous solutions, code, facts, instructions, etc., to solve tasks faster and more reliably in the future. ![Agent 0 Working](/docs/res/ui_screen2.png) 2. **Computer as a Tool**

2. **Computer as a Tool**

- Agent Zero uses the operating system as a tool to accomplish its tasks. It has no single-purpose tools pre-programmed. Instead, it can write its own code and use the terminal to create and use its own tools as needed.
- The only default tools in its arsenal are online search, memory features, communication (with the user and other agents), and code/terminal execution. Everything else is created by the agent itself or can be extended by the user.
- Tool usage functionality has been developed from scratch to be the most compatible and reliable, even with very small models.
- **Default Tools:** Agent Zero includes tools like knowledge, code execution, and communication.
- **Creating Custom Tools:** Extend Agent Zero's functionality by creating your own custom tools.
- **Skills (SKILL.md Standard):** Skills are contextual expertise loaded dynamically when relevant. They use the open SKILL.md standard (developed by Anthropic), making them compatible with Claude Code, Cursor, Goose, OpenAI Codex CLI, and GitHub Copilot.

3. **Multi-agent Cooperation**

- Every agent has a superior agent giving it tasks and instructions. Every agent then reports back to its superior.
- In the case of the first agent in the chain (Agent 0), the superior is the human user; the agent sees no difference.
- Every agent can create its subordinate agent to help break down and solve subtasks. This helps all agents keep their context clean and focused.

![Multi-agent](docs/res/usage/multi-agent.png)

4. **Completely Customizable and Extensible**

- Almost nothing in this framework is hard-coded. Nothing is hidden. Everything can be extended or changed by the user.
- The whole behavior is defined by a system prompt in the **prompts/default/agent.system.md** file. Change this prompt and change the framework dramatically.
- The framework does not guide or limit the agent in any way. There are no hard-coded rails that agents have to follow.
- Every prompt, every small message template sent to the agent in its communication loop can be found in the **prompts/** folder and changed.
- Every default tool can be found in the **python/tools/** folder and changed or copied to create new predefined tools.
- **Automated configuration** via `A0_SET_` environment variables for deployment automation and easy setup.

![Prompts](/docs/res/profiles.png)

5. **Communication is Key**

- Give your agent a proper system prompt and instructions, and it can do miracles.
- Agents can communicate with their superiors and subordinates, asking questions, giving instructions, and providing guidance. Instruct your agents in the system prompt on how to communicate effectively.
- The terminal interface is real-time streamed and interactive. You can stop and intervene at any point. If you see your agent heading in the wrong direction, just stop and tell it right away.
- There is a lot of freedom in this framework. You can instruct your agents to regularly report back to superiors asking for permission to continue. You can instruct them to use point-scoring systems when deciding when to delegate subtasks. Superiors can double-check subordinates' results and dispute. The possibilities are endless.

## 🚀 Real-world use cases

- **Financial Analysis & Charting** - `"Find last month's Bitcoin/USD price trend, correlate with major cryptocurrency news events, generate annotated chart with highlighted key dates"`

- **Excel Automation Pipeline** - `"Scan incoming directory for financial spreadsheets, validate and clean data, consolidate from multiple sources, generate executive reports with flagged anomalies"`

- **API Integration Without Code** - `"Use this Google Gemini API snippet to generate product images, remember the integration for future use"` - agent learns and stores the solution in memory

## 🐳 Fully Dockerized, with Speech-to-Text and TTS

![Settings](docs/res/settings-page-ui1.png)

- Customizable settings allow users to tailor the agent's behavior and responses to their needs.
- The Web UI output is very clean, fluid, colorful, readable, and interactive; nothing is hidden.
- You can load or save chats directly within the Web UI.
- The same output you see in the terminal is automatically saved to an HTML file in **logs/** folder for every session.

![Time example](/docs/res/time_example.jpg)

- Agent output is streamed in real-time, allowing users to read along and intervene at any time.
- No coding is required; only prompting and communication skills are necessary.
- With a solid system prompt, the framework is reliable even with small models, including precise tool usage.

## 👀 Keep in Mind

1. **Agent Zero Can Be Dangerous!**

- With proper instruction, Agent Zero is capable of many things, even potentially dangerous actions concerning your computer, data, or accounts. Always run Agent Zero in an isolated environment (like Docker) and be careful what you wish for.

2. **Agent Zero Is Prompt-based.**

- The whole framework is guided by the **prompts/** folder. Agent guidelines, tool instructions, messages, utility AI functions, it's all there.

## Step 2: Run Agent Zero

### 2.1. Pull the Agent Zero Docker Image

**Using Docker Desktop GUI:**

- Search for `agent0ai/agent-zero` in Docker Desktop
- Click the `Pull` button
- The image will be downloaded to your machine in a few minutes

![docker pull](../res/setup/1-docker-image-search.png)

**Using Terminal:**

```bash
docker pull agent0ai/agent-zero
```

### 2.2. (Optional) Map Folders for Persistence

Choose or create a folder on your computer where Agent Zero will save its data. 

### Setting up persistence is needed only if you want your data and files to remain available even after you delete the container. 

You can pick any location you find convenient:

- **Windows:** `C:\agent-zero-data`
- **macOS/Linux:** `/home/user/agent-zero-data`

You can map just the `/a0/usr` directory (recommended) or individual subfolders of `/a0` to a local directory.

> [!CAUTION]
> Do **not** map the entire `/a0` directory: it contains the application code and can break upgrades.

> [!TIP]
> Choose a location that's easy to access and backup. All your Agent Zero data will be directly accessible in this directory.

### 2.3. Run the Container

**Using Docker Desktop GUI:**

- In Docker Desktop, go to the "Images" tab
- Click the `Run` button next to the `agent0ai/agent-zero` image
- Open the "Optional settings" menu
- **Ensure at least one host port is mapped to container port `80`** (set host port to `0` for automatic assignment)
- Click the `Run` button

![docker port mapping](../res/setup/2-docker-image-run.png)
![docker volume mapping](../res/setup/2-docker-image-run-3.png)

The container will start and show in the "Containers" tab:

![docker containers](../res/setup/4-docker-container-started.png)

### 2.4. Access the Web UI

The framework will take a few seconds to initialize. Find the mapped port in Docker Desktop (shown as `<PORT>:80`) or click the port right under the container ID:

![docker logs](../res/setup/5-docker-click-to-open.png)

Open `http://localhost:<PORT>` in your browser. The Web UI will open - Agent Zero is ready for configuration!

![docker ui](../res/setup/6-docker-a0-running-new.png)

> [!TIP]
> You can also access the Web UI by clicking the port link directly under the container ID in Docker Desktop.

> [!NOTE]
> After starting the container, you'll find all Agent Zero files in your chosen directory. You can access and edit these files directly on your machine, and the changes will be immediately reflected in the running container.

**Running A0 using Terminal?**

```bash
docker run -p 0:80 -v /path/to/your/work_dir:/a0/usr agent0ai/agent-zero
```

- Replace `0` with a fixed port if you prefer (e.g., `50080:80`)

## Step 3: Configure Agent Zero

The UI will show a warning banner "Missing LLM API Key for current settings". Click on `Add your API key` to enter Settings and start configuring A0.

### Settings Configuration

Agent Zero provides a comprehensive settings interface to customize various aspects of its functionality. Access the settings by clicking the "Settings" button with a gear icon in the sidebar.

### Agent Configuration

- **Agent Profile:** Select the agent profile (e.g., `agent0`, `hacker`, `researcher`). Profiles can override prompts, tools, and extensions.
- **Memory Subdirectory:** Select the subdirectory for agent memory storage, allowing separation between different instances.
- **Knowledge Subdirectory:** Specify the location of custom knowledge files to enhance the agent's understanding.

> [!NOTE]
> Since v0.9.7, custom prompts belong in `/a0/agents/<agent_name>/prompts/` rather than a shared `/prompts` folder. See the [Extensions guide](../developer/extensions.md#prompts) for details.

> [!NOTE]
> The Hacker profile is included in the main image. After launch, choose the **hacker** agent profile in Settings if you want the security-focused prompts and tooling. The "hacker" branch is deprecated.

![settings](../res/setup/settings/1-agentConfig.png)

### Chat Model Settings

- **Provider:** Select the chat model provider (e.g., Anthropic)
- **Model Name:** Choose the specific model (e.g., claude-sonnet-4-5)
- **Context Length:** Set the maximum token limit for context window
- **Context Window Space:** Configure how much of the context window is dedicated to chat history

![chat model settings](../res/setup/settings/2-chat-model.png)

**Model naming is provider-specific.**

Use `claude-sonnet-4-5` for Anthropic, but use `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5` for OpenRouter. If you see "Invalid model ID," verify the provider and naming format on the provider website, or search the web for "<name-of-ai-model> model naming".

> [!TIP]
> **Context window tuning:** Set the total context window size first (for example, 100k), then adjust the chat history portion as a fraction of that total. A large fraction on a very large context window can still be enormous.

> [!TIP]
> **API URL:** URL of the API endpoint for the chat model - only needed for some providers like Ollama, LM Studio, Azure, etc.

### Utility Model Configuration

- **Provider & Model:** Select a model for utility tasks like memory organization and summarization
- **Temperature:** Adjust the determinism of utility responses

> [!NOTE]
> Utility models need to be strong enough to extract and consolidate memory reliably. Very small models (e.g., 4B) often fail at this; 70B-class models or high-quality cloud "flash/mini" models work best.

### Embedding Model Settings [Optional]

- **Provider:** Choose the embedding model provider (e.g., OpenAI)
- **Model Name:** Select the specific embedding model (e.g., text-embedding-3-small)

> [!NOTE]
> Agent Zero uses a local embedding model by default (runs on CPU), but you can switch to OpenAI embeddings like `text-embedding-3-small` or `text-embedding-3-large` if preferred.

### Speech to Text Options

- **Model Size:** Choose the speech recognition model size
- **Language Code:** Set the primary language for voice recognition
- **Silence Settings:** Configure silence threshold, duration, and timeout parameters for voice input

### API Keys

Configure API keys for various service providers directly within the Web UI. Click `Save` to confirm your settings.

> [!NOTE]
> **OpenAI API vs Plus subscription:** A ChatGPT Plus subscription does not include API credits. You must provide a separate API key for OpenAI usage in Agent Zero.

> [!TIP]
> For OpenAI-compatible providers (e.g., custom gateways or Z.AI/GLM), add the API key under **External Services → Other OpenAI-compatible API keys**, then select **OpenAI Compatible** as the provider in model settings.

> [!CAUTION]
> **GitHub Copilot Provider:** When using the GitHub Copilot provider, after selecting the model and entering your first prompt, the OAuth login procedure will begin. You'll find the authentication code and link in the output logs. Complete the authentication process by following the provided link and entering the code, then you may continue using Agent Zero.

### Authentication

- **UI Login:** Set username for web interface access

ollama rm <model-name>

## How to Update Agent Zero

> [!NOTE]
> Since v0.9, Agent Zero includes a Backup & Restore workflow in the Settings UI. This is the **safest** way to upgrade Docker instances.

### Recommended Update Process (Docker)

1. **Keep the old container running** and note its port.
2. **Pull the new image** (`agent0ai/agent-zero:latest`).
3. **Start a new container** on a different host port.
4. In the **old** instance, open **Settings → Backup & Restore** and create a backup.
5. In the **new** instance, restore that backup from the same panel.

> [!TIP]
> If the new instance fails to load settings, remove `/a0/usr/settings.json` and restart to regenerate default settings.

## Using Agent Zero on Your Mobile Device

Agent Zero can be accessed from mobile devices and other computers using the built-in **Tunnel feature**.

### Recommended: Using Tunnel (Remote Access)

The Tunnel feature allows secure access to your Agent Zero instance from anywhere:

1. Open Settings in the Web UI
2. Navigate to the **External Services** tab
3. Click on **Flare Tunnel** in the navigation menu
4. Click **Create Tunnel** to generate a secure HTTPS URL
5. Share this URL to access Agent Zero from any device

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security:** Always set a username and password in Settings → Authentication before creating a tunnel to secure your instance on the internet.

For complete details on tunnel configuration and security considerations, see the [Remote Access via Tunneling](../guides/usage.md#remote-access-via-tunneling) section in the Usage Guide.

### Alternative: Local Network Access

If you prefer to keep access limited to your local network:

1. Find the mapped port in Docker Desktop (format: `<PORT>:80`, e.g., `32771:80`)
2. Access from the same computer: `http://localhost:<PORT>`
3. Access from other devices on the network: `http://<YOUR_COMPUTER_IP>:<PORT>`

> [!TIP]
> Find your computer's IP address with `ipconfig` (Windows) or `ifconfig`/`ip addr` (macOS/Linux). It's usually in the format `192.168.x.x` or `10.0.x.x`.

## Advanced: Automated Configuration via Environment Variables

Agent Zero settings can be automatically configured using environment variables with the `A0_SET_` prefix in your `.env` file. This enables automated deployments without manual configuration.

**Usage:**

Add variables to your `.env` file in the format:

```env
A0_SET_{setting_name}={value}
```

**Examples:**

```env
# Model configuration
A0_SET_chat_model_provider=anthropic
A0_SET_chat_model_name=claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022
A0_SET_chat_model_ctx_length=200000

# Memory settings
A0_SET_memory_recall_enabled=true
A0_SET_memory_recall_interval=5

# Agent configuration
A0_SET_agent_profile=custom
A0_SET_agent_memory_subdir=production
```

**Docker usage:**

When running Docker, you can pass these as environment variables:

```bash
docker run -p 50080:80 \
  -e A0_SET_chat_model_provider=anthropic \
  -e A0_SET_chat_model_name=claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022 \
  agent0ai/agent-zero
```

**Notes:**

- These provide initial default values when settings.json doesn't exist or when new settings are added to the application. Once a value is saved in settings.json, it takes precedence over these environment variables.
- Sensitive settings (API keys, passwords) use their existing environment variables
- Container/process restart required for changes to take effect

---

### Manual Migration (Legacy or Non-Docker)

If you are migrating from older, non-Docker setups, A0 handles the migration of legacy folders and files automatically at runtime. The right place to save your files and directories is `a0/usr`.

After following the instructions for your specific operating system, you should have Agent Zero successfully installed and running. You can now start exploring the framework's capabilities and experimenting with creating your own intelligent agents.

I believe that “Strict” is constraint theater; “secure” is engineered autonomy.
Strict is constraint theater. Secure autonomy is engineered autonomy.

For a 100% autonomous system, the correct model is:
- **Identity-first:** every agent and every action has verifiable identity, scoped authority, and explicit delegation boundaries.
- **Capability-scoped execution:** tools run with least privilege through a broker; no blanket shell access, no ambient credentials, no uncontrolled filesystem or network reach.
- **Continuous verification:** every action is a closed loop: execute → measure → validate → self-correct → retry; escalate only when policy boundaries are reached.
- **Full provenance:** tamper-evident, immutable event records for plans, tool calls, decisions, artifacts, and outcomes; complete artifact lineage and replayability.
- **Bounded blast radius:** sandboxing by default, risk-tier approval classes for destructive operations, canary/transactional changes, and fast rollback as a first-class requirement.
- **Runtime drift detection:** configuration and state divergence is treated as an incident with automated containment, diagnosis, and remediation loops.

**Next best thing: Proof-carrying autonomy.**  
No action “counts” unless it produces an evidence bundle that a deterministic policy engine can accept. Each action must ship with: testable claims, scoped capabilities, bounded execution, verification proofs (tests/probes/scans/diffs), a commit-or-rollback decision, and a signed provenance chain.

Operate from secure-autonomy principles with proof-carrying actions, not strict-mode gatekeeping.

Here’s a clear, buildable “how to” for creating an OpenClaw Skill that works with Squibler **today**.
### What we know from research

- Squibler is accessible via a standard web login flow. ([Squibler](https://www.squibler.io/auth/login/?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Login"))
- OpenClaw Skills are **folders** containing a `SKILL.md` with **YAML frontmatter + instructions** (AgentSkills-compatible). ([OpenClaw](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/skills?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Skills"))
- I could not find any official public “Squibler Developer API” documentation; the practical path is **browser automation** (Playwright) or export/import.

---
## The recommended approach

**OpenClaw Skill (thin)** → calls a **local Squibler Connector** (Playwright) that does the real work.

This keeps your Skill stable even if you later switch implementations (API appears later, UI changes, etc.).

---
# Step-by-step: Squibler Skill via a local Connector

## 1) Create a small local service: `squibler-connector`

This service exposes simple HTTP endpoints like:

- `POST /login`
- `GET /projects`
- `POST /projects/:id/docs`
- `POST /docs/:id/append`
- `GET /docs/:id`
- `POST /docs/:id/export`

Internally, it uses Playwright to:

- open Squibler login ([Squibler](https://www.squibler.io/auth/login/?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Login"))
- authenticate (email login or Google login)
- navigate to project/doc
- read or write editor content
- trigger export/download (if the UI supports it)

**Key design rule:** keep the Connector API “document-centric” (get doc, append text, export). That maps cleanly to writing workflows.

### Security baseline (don’t skip)

- Put Squibler credentials in environment variables or a secrets store.
- Redact logs.
- Run the connector in a locked-down container, and restrict outbound network to Squibler domains only.

---

## 2) Create the OpenClaw Skill folder

OpenClaw expects a skill directory with a `SKILL.md` that teaches the agent how to use it. ([OpenClaw](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/skills?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Skills"))

Example folder:

```
skills/
  squibler/
    SKILL.md
```

---

## 3) Write `SKILL.md` (copy/paste template)

This is a practical template that matches how OpenClaw describes Skills (frontmatter + instructions). ([OpenClaw](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/skills?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Skills"))

```md
---
name: squibler
description: "Create, update, and export Squibler drafts via a local Squibler Connector service."
tools:
  - http
---

POST /projects/{projectId}/docs
Body JSON:
{ "title": "Chapter 1", "type": "chapter" }

### Append text to a doc
POST /docs/{docId}/append
Body JSON:
{ "text": "..." }

### Replace doc text (use sparingly)
POST /docs/{docId}/replace
Body JSON:
{ "text": "..." }

### Export a doc
POST /docs/{docId}/export
Body JSON:
{ "format": "docx" }

## Rules
- Prefer APPEND over REPLACE.
- Always read the current doc before making major changes.
- If export fails, return the connector error verbatim.
- Never include credentials in any request payloads or logs.
```

This makes the agent behave consistently and reduces accidental overwrites.

---

## 4) Run it locally (fastest path)

- Run the connector on `localhost:8787`.
    
- Confirm it can:
    
    1. login
    2. list projects
    3. open a doc
    4. append text
    5. export

Once that works, the OpenClaw side becomes trivial: it’s just HTTP calls.

## Purpose

This note defines what the application must do, for whom, and what success looks like.

## Product Goal

Build a local-first, autonomous AI operations platform that can run AI businesses, apps, and automations with very small human teams.

## Primary Users

- George (owner/operator)
- Dottie (frontline assistant runtime)
- Einstein (Creative Technologist)
- Polly/OpenClaw (orchestration and delegated execution)

## Core Outcomes

- Reliable multi-channel operation (OpenWebUI, Telegram, Discord)
- Durable memory and retrieval (SQLite + Weaviate + Neo4j)
- Safe autonomous execution with clear evidence and rollback paths
- Comprehensive voice support (transcription and speech pathways)

## Core Runtime Requirements (Aligned to Current Implementation)

- Dottie gateway is the session-memory owner for OpenWebUI and Telegram continuity.
- Durable memory follows SQLite-first canonical writes with outbox sync to Weaviate and Neo4j.
- Includes GitHub sidecar API, heartbeat scheduler, and backup service.
- OpenWebUI code interpreter must run on internal Docker-network Jupyter, not browser-only execution.
- Docker services encompass OpenWebUI, OpenClaw, Weaviate, Neo4j, n8n, SearXNG, and code-interpreter.
- OpenWebUI upgrades must remain safe by keeping customization in compose/env/sidecar configuration (no OpenWebUI core fork).

## Natural-Language Tooling Requirement (Dottie + Polly)

- Both agents must infer when tools are needed from plain-language intent.
- Operators should not need explicit trigger phrases for memory/search/execution flows.
- If intent is ambiguous, agent asks one concise clarifying question.
- If no tool is needed for a reliable answer, respond directly without unnecessary tool calls.

## Acceptance Criteria (Current Baseline)

- Same chat session continuity works in OpenWebUI without client-side history dependency.
- Explicit durable save/recall works across surfaces (OpenWebUI <-> Telegram).
- OpenWebUI interpreter E2E passes for imports, file roundtrip, and concurrency.

# Indexed: 2026-02-27T05:18:36.520832+00:00

## Local Ollama Configuration and Networking Fixes

After the initial setup, we overhauled Agent Zero to completely bypass OpenRouter and route all LLM traffic through the local Ollama proxy.

1. **LiteLLM Networking:** We injected `OLLAMA_API_BASE=http://host.docker.internal:11434` into the environment. Agent Zero uses LiteLLM under the hood, and this standard environment variable forces LiteLLM to route API requests to the host machine rather than the container's isolated `localhost`.
2. **Container Host Resolution:** We added `extra_hosts: - "host.docker.internal:host-gateway"` so the Agent Zero container could perform DNS resolution for the host machine.
3. **Environment Overrides:** We used `A0_SET_*` environment variables in `docker-compose.yml` to programmatically force the internal models to the following:
    - **Chat Model:** `minimax-m2.5:cloud`
    - **Utility Model:** `kimi-k2.5:cloud` (pulled securely via the Ollama proxy)
    - **Web Browser Model:** `minimax-m2.5:cloud`
    - **Embedding Model:** `qwen3-embedding:8b`

## Result

The Agent Zero container (`ajf-agent-zero`) is now fully operational and securely communicating with the local Ollama gateway. E2E browser tests confirm the "Missing LLM API Key" warnings are completely resolved, and the AI correctly processes and responds to chat inputs using your custom `kimi` and `minimax` models.

# Glossary - Plain English

Quick definitions for this project.

## Dottie

Your frontline assistant runtime. Handles user interactions, tool execution, memory use, and day-to-day operation.

## Polly

OpenClaw orchestration layer used for delegated complex execution and sub-agent coordination.

## OpenWebUI Gateway

A local compatibility layer that lets OpenWebUI talk to Dottie using OpenAI-style APIs.

## Agentic loop

Model thinks, calls tools, gets results, and continues until it can answer.

## SQLite memory

Local durable memory database. Fast and always available.

## Weaviate

Vector database for semantic memory search (meaning-based retrieval).

## Neo4j

Graph database for entity relationships and contextual links.

## Heartbeat

Scheduled autonomous actions (reminders/checks) run by the scheduler.

## BackupService

Creates local backups of important runtime state (`memory.db`, `heartbeat.db`, `.env`).

## Vault path drift

When configured vault path and runtime-used vault path are not the same.

## Proof-carrying autonomy

Autonomous action only counts when it includes evidence of what happened and why it is valid.

# Build Task: jooseyVision for Meta Ray-Ban Glasses

## Objective

Build an end-to-end capability so Meta Ray-Ban glasses can be used as a hands-free Vision interface across George’s ecosystem:

- **OpenWebUI Vision** as an operator-facing option for vision-enabled conversations.
- An additional integration into **Ai joose Factory** (the project living in the `/src` folder) so the same glasses-driven vision capability can be used within that codebase.

This must scale to **any agent** (present and future), not just Dottie/Einstein.

---
## Scope

### In scope

- Fork and extend **VisionClaw** (Meta Ray-Ban glasses client) to support George’s environment and routing needs. This will be a Public GitHub project and I will be submitting PRs to VisionClaw in hopes to be added as a contributor.
- Integrate the glasses experience into **OpenWebUI** so it can be used with OpenWebUI’s Vision workflows (operator-facing).
- Add the same capability into **Ai joose Factory (`/src`)** as an integration deliverable (implementation details determined by the codebase owner). This is to support other connectible clients akin to Telegram, Discord, etc.
- Support a scalable multi-agent routing contract that works for unlimited agents.
- Codex will determine the correct placement and flow.
### Out of scope (this phase)

- Deep UI redesign of OpenWebUI.
- Broad refactors of Ai joose Factory beyond what is required for integration.

---
## Current State (Known Inputs)

- OpenWebUI is running and already connected to a gateway via `/v1/chat/completions`.
- Ai joose Factory exists in the `/src` folder and will receive an integration.
- OpenClaw container is running and available for tool/action delegation.
- George uses an Ollama-based model setup:
    
    - **Primary reasoning:** `kimi-k2.5:cloud`
    - **Vision fallback:** `qwen3-vl` local

---
## Required End-State Behavior

### A) Glasses-driven Vision UX (via OpenWebUI)

- George can use Meta Ray-Ban glasses as a practical vision input method for OpenWebUI.
- OpenWebUI remains an operator-facing interface for chat, organization, and model selection.
- Vision interactions behave predictably in chats and channels.
### B) Glasses-driven Vision Capability (via Ai joose Factory `/src`)

- Ai joose Factory must be able to consume or otherwise integrate the same glasses vision stream/event feed.
- The exact placement, module boundaries, and responsibilities inside `/src` are implementation-defined by Codex based on the codebase.

### C) Any-agent routing and persistence

- The operator can target any agent by:
    
    - “Switch to `<agent>`”
    - “@agent …”

- Agent selection persists per session until changed.
- Unknown agent names result in a clear fallback and prompt to clarify.
### D) Model and fallback behavior

- Default reasoning model: `kimi-k2.5:cloud`
- Vision understanding fallback: `qwen3-vl`
- Fallback must be deterministic and logged (what happened and why).
### E) Delegation to OpenClaw for actions

- When an action is required, delegate to OpenClaw.
- Delegated action must route to the correct agent identity (based on active agent selection).

---
## Integration Requirements

### 1) VisionClaw fork requirements

- Must be runnable in George’s environment.
- Must support configuration for:
    
    - Ollama URL / endpoints
    - Primary model id
    - Vision fallback model id
    - OpenClaw gateway URL
    - OpenClaw auth token
    - Default agent id
    
- Must provide a usable interface/output suitable for integration with:
    - OpenWebUI
    - Ai joose Factory (`/src`)
### 2) OpenWebUI integration requirements
lGCtD0A6Rq:
- Glasses vision input must be usable from within OpenWebUI’s normal workflows.
- Integration should avoid forking OpenWebUI unless unavoidable.
- Must support controlled usage in:
    
    - chats (vision inputs usable in conversation)
    - channels (optional, with guardrails to prevent noise)
### 3) Ai joose Factory (`/src`) integration requirements

- Add an integration deliverable inside `/src` that enables Ai joose Factory to use glasses-driven vision input.
- Exact internal wiring is determined by Codex based on the repository’s existing patterns and constraints.
- Must align with the same agent routing contract and logging expectations described in this document.

---
## Non-Negotiables

- Supports **unlimited agents** (no hard-coded Dottie/Einstein logic).
- Prevents **cross-agent bleed** (agent-scoped session context).
- Clear, reproducible configuration (single source of truth via `.env` or equivalent).
- Operational logs must include:
    
    - active agent
    - model used (primary vs fallback)
    - vision usage events
    - tool delegation events

- Must work with George’s model stack (primary + fallback) as stated.

---
## Deliverables

- Forked VisionClaw repo under org control.
- OpenWebUI integration layer (documented, minimal surface area).
- Ai joose Factory `/src` integration deliverable (documented).
- Routing contract documentation:
    
    - how to switch agents
    - how to do one-shot targeting
    - persistence rules
        
- Validation checklist (manual + basic automated tests where feasible).

---
## Acceptance Criteria

- George can use Meta Ray-Ban glasses to drive vision interactions through **OpenWebUI OR Ai joose Factory (`/src`)**.

- The system supports **any agent** (present and future) via the routing contract:
    
    - “Switch to `<agent>`” persists per session
    - “@agent …” routes one-shot requests correctly
    
- Vision fallback to `qwen3-vl` works reliably when needed.
- Actions are delegated through OpenClaw and executed by the selected agent.
- Restarting services preserves expected behavior (no drift, no random agent switching).

---
## Engineering Notes: “1%” Considerations (What to Watch For)

### 1) Identity and attribution must be unambiguous

- Always log: `session_id`, `active_agent_id`, `requested_agent_id` (if any), `model_used`, and whether vision fallback ran.
- In OpenWebUI, don’t rely on “model meta tags” alone as proof of identity. If attribution matters, make agent identity explicit in the payload/metadata you control.
### 2) Make agent routing deterministic

- Pick one canonical voice-friendly pattern: “Switch to `<agent>`”.
- Treat “@agent …” as secondary (better for typed input).
- For unknown agents: return the list of available agents and ask the operator to choose.
### 3) Define the session boundary and persist it

- Define session identity (e.g., device id + user id + time window).
- Persist `active_agent_id` per session.
- Provide an explicit reset command (“reset session”).
### 4) Treat vision as a stream, not just attachments

- Decide what is stored and forwarded:
    
    - last frame only, sampled frames, or visual summary packets only.
	- Avoid posting raw frames into channels by default.
### 5) Make the vision fallback a first-class pipeline

- Define a “visual context packet” format (short description, detected text, scene summary, uncertainties).
- Feed that packet into the reasoning step.
- Log when and why fallback triggered.
### 6) Tool delegation must be constrained and auditable

- Use an explicit “action intent” trigger or confirmation pattern for destructive actions.
- Log tool calls with: agent id, request text, tool name, parameters, outcome.
- Prevent loops where the assistant triggers tools repeatedly from its own output.
### 7) Add rate limiting and anti-spam controls

- Frame throttling plus hard caps per minute.
- Debounce identical requests.
- Add “quiet mode” and “status mode.”
### 8) Never leak secrets
vdCLMopYrS:
- Frame throttling plus hard caps per minute.
- Debounce identical requests.
- Add “quiet mode” and “status mode.”
### 8) Never leak secrets

- Redact OpenClaw tokens and gateway credentials at the logger level.
    
- Ensure errors don’t dump headers or env vars.
    

### 9) Assume network failures

- Define behavior when phone/VPN/Wi-Fi drops: fail gracefully, and choose whether to queue or discard pending actions.
    
- Provide reconnect logic and a health check.
    

### 10) Expect compatibility drift

- Keep OpenWebUI changes minimal/external where possible.
    
- Version/pin what matters (VisionClaw fork, model tags, gateway behavior).
    
- Maintain a smoke test checklist after upgrades.
    

### 11) Keep operator UX crisp

- Provide a “Status” command that reports: active agent, model, vision on/off, last tool action.
    

### 12) Define success metrics

Examples:

- Median response time for a vision query
    
- Tool delegation success rate
    
- Agent-routing misroute rate
    
- Count of “operator confusion” events (status prompts, resets)
    

**Minimum required notes for engineering execution**

- Deterministic routing rules + unknown-agent handling
    
- Session definition + persistence rules
    
- Visual context packet format + logging
    
- Tool delegation guardrails + audit logging

# Build Task: jooseyVision for Meta Ray-Ban Glasses

**Project Type:** Internal
**Project:** Ai joose Factory Application
**Build Task:** jooseyVision for Meta Ray-Ban Glasses
**Source Code:** /src

## Objective

Build an end-to-end capability so Meta Ray-Ban glasses can be used as a hands-free Vision interface across George’s ecosystem:

- **OpenWebUI Vision** as an operator-facing option for vision-enabled conversations.
- An additional integration into **Ai joose Factory** (the project living in the `/src` folder) so the same glasses-driven vision capability can be used within that codebase.

This must scale to **any agent** (present and future), not just Dottie/Einstein.

---
## Scope

### In scope

- Fork and extend **VisionClaw** (Meta Ray-Ban glasses client) to support George’s environment and routing needs. This will be a Public GitHub project and I will be submitting PRs to VisionClaw in hopes to be added as a contributor.
- Integrate the glasses experience into **OpenWebUI** so it can be used with OpenWebUI’s Vision workflows (operator-facing).
- Add the same capability into **Ai joose Factory (`/src`)** as an integration deliverable (implementation details determined by the codebase owner). This is to support other connectible clients akin to Telegram, Discord, etc.
- Support a scalable multi-agent routing contract that works for unlimited agents.
- Codex will determine the correct placement and flow.
### Out of scope (this phase)

- Deep UI redesign of OpenWebUI.
- Broad refactors of Ai joose Factory beyond what is required for integration.

---
## Current State (Known Inputs)

- OpenWebUI is running and already connected to a gateway via `/v1/chat/completions`.
- Ai joose Factory exists in the `/src` folder and will receive an integration.
- OpenClaw container is running and available for tool/action delegation.
- George uses an Ollama-based model setup:
    
    - **Primary reasoning:** `kimi-k2.5:cloud`
    - **Vision fallback:** `qwen3-vl` local

---
## Required End-State Behavior

### A) Glasses-driven Vision UX (via OpenWebUI)

- George can use Meta Ray-Ban glasses as a practical vision input method for OpenWebUI.
- OpenWebUI remains an operator-facing interface for chat, organization, and model selection.
- Vision interactions behave predictably in chats and channels.
### B) Glasses-driven Vision Capability (via Ai joose Factory `/src`)

- Ai joose Factory must be able to consume or otherwise integrate the same glasses vision stream/event feed.
- The exact placement, module boundaries, and responsibilities inside `/src` are implementation-defined by Codex based on the codebase.

### C) Any-agent routing and persistence

- The operator can target any agent by:
    
    - “Switch to `<agent>`”
    - “@agent …”

- Agent selection persists per session until changed.
- Unknown agent names result in a clear fallback and prompt to clarify.
### D) Model and fallback behavior

- Default reasoning model: `kimi-k2.5:cloud`
- Vision understanding fallback: `qwen3-vl`
- Fallback must be deterministic and logged (what happened and why).
### E) Delegation to OpenClaw for actions

- When an action is required, delegate to OpenClaw.
- Delegated action must route to the correct agent identity (based on active agent selection).

---
## Integration Requirements

### 1) VisionClaw fork requirements

- Must be runnable in George’s environment.
- Must support configuration for:
    
    - Ollama URL / endpoints
    - Primary model id
    - Vision fallback model id
    - OpenClaw gateway URL
    - OpenClaw auth token
    - Default agent id
    
- Must provide a usable interface/output suitable for integration with:
    - OpenWebUI
    - Ai joose Factory (`/src`)
### 2) OpenWebUI integration requirements
O3RcIxCS1p:
- Glasses vision input must be usable from within OpenWebUI’s normal workflows.
- Integration should avoid forking OpenWebUI unless unavoidable.
- Must support controlled usage in:
    
    - chats (vision inputs usable in conversation)
    - channels (optional, with guardrails to prevent noise)
### 3) Ai joose Factory (`/src`) integration requirements

- Add an integration deliverable inside `/src` that enables Ai joose Factory to use glasses-driven vision input.
- Exact internal wiring is determined by Codex based on the repository’s existing patterns and constraints.
- Must align with the same agent routing contract and logging expectations described in this document.

---
## Non-Negotiables

- Supports **unlimited agents** (no hard-coded Dottie/Einstein logic).
- Prevents **cross-agent bleed** (agent-scoped session context).
- Clear, reproducible configuration (single source of truth via `.env` or equivalent).
- Operational logs must include:
    
    - active agent
    - model used (primary vs fallback)
    - vision usage events
    - tool delegation events

- Must work with George’s model stack (primary + fallback) as stated.

---
## Deliverables

- Forked VisionClaw repo under org control.
- OpenWebUI integration layer (documented, minimal surface area).
- Ai joose Factory `/src` integration deliverable (documented).
- Routing contract documentation:
    
    - how to switch agents
    - how to do one-shot targeting
    - persistence rules
        
- Validation checklist (manual + basic automated tests where feasible).

---
## Acceptance Criteria

- George can use Meta Ray-Ban glasses to drive vision interactions through **OpenWebUI OR Ai joose Factory (`/src`)**.

- The system supports **any agent** (present and future) via the routing contract:
    
    - “Switch to `<agent>`” persists per session
    - “@agent …” routes one-shot requests correctly
    
- Vision fallback to `qwen3-vl` works reliably when needed.
- Actions are delegated through OpenClaw and executed by the selected agent.
- Restarting services preserves expected behavior (no drift, no random agent switching).

---
## Engineering Notes: “1%” Considerations (What to Watch For)

### 1) Identity and attribution must be unambiguous

- Always log: `session_id`, `active_agent_id`, `requested_agent_id` (if any), `model_used`, and whether vision fallback ran.
- In OpenWebUI, don’t rely on “model meta tags” alone as proof of identity. If attribution matters, make agent identity explicit in the payload/metadata you control.
### 2) Make agent routing deterministic

- Pick one canonical voice-friendly pattern: “Switch to `<agent>`”.
- Treat “@agent …” as secondary (better for typed input).
- For unknown agents: return the list of available agents and ask the operator to choose.
### 3) Define the session boundary and persist it

- Define session identity (e.g., device id + user id + time window).
- Persist `active_agent_id` per session.
- Provide an explicit reset command (“reset session”).
### 4) Treat vision as a stream, not just attachments

- Decide what is stored and forwarded:
    
    - last frame only, sampled frames, or visual summary packets only.
	- Avoid posting raw frames into channels by default.
### 5) Make the vision fallback a first-class pipeline

- Define a “visual context packet” format (short description, detected text, scene summary, uncertainties).
- Feed that packet into the reasoning step.
- Log when and why fallback triggered.
### 6) Tool delegation must be constrained and auditable

- Use an explicit “action intent” trigger or confirmation pattern for destructive actions.
- Log tool calls with: agent id, request text, tool name, parameters, outcome.
- Prevent loops where the assistant triggers tools repeatedly from its own output.
### 7) Add rate limiting and anti-spam controls

- Frame throttling plus hard caps per minute.
- Debounce identical requests.
- Add “quiet mode” and “status mode.”
### 8) Never leak secrets
XnULJEMELA:
- Frame throttling plus hard caps per minute.
- Debounce identical requests.
- Add “quiet mode” and “status mode.”
### 8) Never leak secrets

- Redact OpenClaw tokens and gateway credentials at the logger level.
    
- Ensure errors don’t dump headers or env vars.
    

### 9) Assume network failures

- Define behavior when phone/VPN/Wi-Fi drops: fail gracefully, and choose whether to queue or discard pending actions.
    
- Provide reconnect logic and a health check.
    

### 10) Expect compatibility drift

- Keep OpenWebUI changes minimal/external where possible.
    
- Version/pin what matters (VisionClaw fork, model tags, gateway behavior).
    
- Maintain a smoke test checklist after upgrades.
    

### 11) Keep operator UX crisp

- Provide a “Status” command that reports: active agent, model, vision on/off, last tool action.
    

### 12) Define success metrics

Examples:

- Median response time for a vision query
    
- Tool delegation success rate
    
- Agent-routing misroute rate
    
- Count of “operator confusion” events (status prompts, resets)
    

**Minimum required notes for engineering execution**

- Deterministic routing rules + unknown-agent handling
    
- Session definition + persistence rules
    
- Visual context packet format + logging
    
- Tool delegation guardrails + audit logging

You are helping me build **Ai joose Factory** — a personal AI agent inspired by the OpenClaw project (formerly ClawdBot/Moltbot).

## 📖 Your First Task

Before writing any code, familiarize yourself with the OpenClaw project:

- Read the OpenClaw GitHub repo (`github.com/steipete/openclaw`) to understand its architecture: messaging channels, agentic tool loop, memory system, skills, proactive heartbeat
- Understand what it does well: multi-LLM support, persistent memory, voice, 700+ community skills, proactive check-ins
- Understand its weaknesses: exposed web server (42K+ instances found public), untrusted community skills (341 found malicious), per-token API costs ($500–$5K/mo reported), massive codebase that users clone but never read

---

## 🏗️ What We're Building

A **lean, secure, fully-understood** version of the same concept. Not a fork — built from scratch so I understand every line.

### Architecture Principles

|Principle|Detail|
|---|---|
|**TypeScript**|ES modules, modular folder structure|
|**Telegram-only**|No web server, no exposed ports, no HTTP endpoints|
|**Security by default**|Telegram user ID whitelist, `.env` secrets only, no hardcoded keys|
|**Agentic loop**|LLM can call tools, get results, call more tools (max iterations with safety limit)|
|**MCP for integrations**|Model Context Protocol servers instead of community skill files. No untrusted code.|
|**Local-first**|Everything runs on my machine. Data never leaves unless I explicitly connect an external service.|

### Core Tech Stack

|Package|Purpose|
|---|---|
|`grammy`|Telegram bot framework|
|`@anthropic-ai/sdk`|Claude (primary LLM)|
|`better-sqlite3` • FTS5|Persistent memory|
|`openai` SDK|Whisper transcription|
|ElevenLabs API|Text-to-speech|
|`tsx`|Dev runner, TypeScript strict mode|

### 🔒 Security Requirements (Non-Negotiable)

> ⚠️ These are hard requirements. Never compromise on any of these.

1. **User ID whitelist** — only respond to my Telegram user ID. Silently ignore everyone else.
2. **No web server** — use Telegram long-polling, never expose a port.
3. **Secrets in `.env` only** — never in code, never in memory files, never in logs.
4. **Tool safety** — dangerous shell commands require confirmation. Max iteration limit on the agent loop.
5. **No third-party skill files** — integrations via MCP only (standardized, auditable, separate process).

---

## 🚀 Build Approach

We'll build this in levels. Start with Level 1 and I'll tell you when to move to the next.

- Level 1 — Foundation
    
    Telegram bot + LLM + basic agent loop (one tool: `get_current_time`)
    
- Level 2 — Memory
    
    Persistent memory (SQLite + FTS5 + memory tools)
    
- Level 3 — Voice
    
    Voice messages (Whisper in, ElevenLabs out)
    
- Level 4 — Tools
    
    Tools + MCP bridge (shell, files, external services)
    
- Level 5 — Heartbeat
    
    Proactive morning briefing, scheduled check-ins

# Ai joose Factory Application Summary Report
## What it is currently?
Ai joose Factory is a local-first AI operations app that combines an OpenAI-compatible gateway, multi-channel agent interfaces, and a hybrid memory stack. It is implemented as a TypeScript runtime (`src/index.ts`) plus supporting Docker services (`docker-compose.yml`).

## Who it is for?
Primary persona: George (owner/operator), with Dottie as Default Managing Agent and Einstein, a (Creative Technologist) Agent runtime and Polly as an OpenClaw orchestrator (`APPLICATION_PRD.md`).

## What it does currently.
- Exposes an OpenAI-compatible gateway with chat and model endpoints (`/v1/chat/completions`, `/v1/models`) in `src/gateway.ts`.
- Supports multiple user surfaces: OpenWebUI, Telegram, and Discord (`docker-compose.yml`, `src/bot.ts`, `src/discord.ts`).
- Persists conversation continuity with a gateway session store in SQLite (`data/gateway_sessions.db`, `src/session-store.ts`).
- Runs hybrid memory with SQLite as source-of-truth plus async Weaviate and Neo4j sync (`src/memory/index.ts`, `src/memory/sqlite.ts`).
- Provides voice pathways for transcription and speech (`/v1/audio/transcriptions`, `/v1/audio/speech`) in `src/gateway.ts`.
- Enforces access controls such as Telegram user whitelist and optional Discord guild whitelist (`src/bot.ts`, `src/discord.ts`, `src/config.ts`).

## How it works today. (architecture)
- Runtime core: `src/index.ts` starts GitHub sidecar API, memory service, heartbeat scheduler, Dottie gateway, backup service, Telegram bot, and optional Discord bot.
- Service layer: Docker composes OpenWebUI (`:3005`), OpenClaw (`:3000`), Weaviate (`:8080`), Neo4j (`:7687`), n8n (`:5679`), SearXNG (`:8081`), and code-interpreter (`docker-compose.yml`).
- Data flow:
  1. OpenWebUI/Telegram -> Gateway (`src/gateway.ts`) -> Agent -> tools + memory -> response.
  2. Discord -> Agent directly (`src/discord.ts`) -> tools + memory -> response.
  3. Memory writes: SQLite first, then outbox-driven async sync to Weaviate and Neo4j (`src/memory/index.ts`).

## How to run (minimal)
1. Ensure required `.env` values are set (for example `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN`, `TELEGRAM_USER_ID_WHITELIST`, `OLLAMA_CLOUD_API_KEY`; see `src/config.ts`).
2. Start core containers: `docker compose up -d`.
3. Start app runtime from repo root: `npm run dev`.
4. Quick checks: `curl http://localhost:8000/v1/models` and `curl http://localhost:3005/api/config` (`Ai joose Factory BrainTWO/DEVELOPMENT/Runbook - Local Operations.md`).

## Not found in repo
- A single canonical first-time bootstrap guide (end-to-end install + all required env vars in one place).

# Polly(OpenClaw) Lead Dev Agent Manages Other Agents

### Executive Summary

This document defines how an OpenClaw multi-agent environment operates when a **Main Agent (Dev Lead)** is responsible for supervising a **core team** (e.g., Senior Software Developer, QA) while still allowing any agent to spawn **unique specialist agents** as needed to complete work “like a polymath.” The system is governed by a clear authority model: the Dev Lead owns delivery and acceptance, agents operate within explicit scopes, and dynamic specialists are created through a controlled, auditable spawn process. The outcome is high autonomy without chaos: work gets done quickly, safely, and verifiably, even when tasks require new, one-off expertise.

OpenClaw supports this via **nested sub-agents** when you enable it:

- Default is **depth 1** (main can spawn sub-agents). ([OpenClaw](https://docs.openclaw.ai/concepts/session-tool "Session Tools - OpenClaw"))
    
- If you set `maxSpawnDepth: 2`, then a **depth-1 orchestrator sub-agent** can spawn **depth-2 worker sub-sub-agents** (a Polymath-style orchestrator pattern). ([OpenClaw](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/subagents "Sub-Agents - OpenClaw"))
    
- When `maxSpawnDepth >= 2`, OpenClaw gives the orchestrator sub-agent the needed **session tools** (including `sessions_spawn`) so it can **manage its children**. ([OpenClaw](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/subagents "Sub-Agents - OpenClaw"))
    

### How to model your “Polymath” behavior in OpenClaw terms

- **Main Agent = Supervisor** (spawns orchestrators, monitors, can stop/cascade stop) ([OpenClaw](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/subagents "Sub-Agents - OpenClaw"))
    
- **Orchestrator Sub-Agent (depth 1) = Delegator** (spawns workers when `maxSpawnDepth: 2`) ([OpenClaw](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/subagents "Sub-Agents - OpenClaw"))
    
- **Worker Sub-Sub-Agents (depth 2) = Specialists** (execute bounded tasks; no further spawning) ([OpenClaw](https://docs.openclaw.ai/tools/subagents "Sub-Agents - OpenClaw"))
    

If you want “spawn _specific_ agents,” OpenClaw’s `sessions_spawn` supports targeting an `agentId` when allowed via `agents.list[].subagents.allowAgents`, so the supervisor can explicitly choose which agent identity runs the work. ## Supervisor-Managed Teams With Dynamic Specialist Spawning

### Executive Summary

This document defines how an OpenClaw multi-agent environment operates when a **Main Agent (Dev Lead)** is responsible for supervising a **core team** (e.g., Senior Software Developer, QA) while still allowing any agent to spawn **unique specialist agents** as needed to complete work “like a polymath.” The system is governed by a clear authority model: the Dev Lead owns delivery and acceptance, agents operate within explicit scopes, and dynamic specialists are created through a controlled, auditable spawn process. The outcome is high autonomy without chaos: work gets done quickly, safely, and verifiably, even when tasks require new, one-off expertise.

---

## 1) Purpose

Establish a consistent operating model for:

- A **Main Agent** acting as Dev Lead (“boss” by capability, not title)
    
- A **core roster** of persistent agents (Senior Dev, QA, etc.)
    
- **Dynamic specialist agents** that can be created on demand
    
- Bounded autonomy using spawn depth, allowlists, scope, and acceptance gates
    

---

## 2) Definitions

- **Main Agent (Dev Lead):** Primary supervisor. Owns planning, delegation, integration, and “done” decisions.
    
- **Core Agents:** Persistent roles with stable responsibilities (e.g., Senior Dev, QA).
    
- **Dynamic Specialist Agents:** Ephemeral, single-purpose agents spawned to cover unique expertise gaps.
    
- **Spawn Depth:** Maximum nesting of agent creation (e.g., depth 1 means only Dev Lead spawns; depth 2 means spawned agents can spawn workers).
    
- **Scope:** Explicit allowed tools, files, services, and actions for an agent.
    

---

## 3) Authority and Responsibility Model

### 3.1 Dev Lead Authority

The Dev Lead is the only agent that may:

- Declare work **complete**
    
- Approve merges/releases/shipping outcomes
    
- Expand the project scope
    
- Promote a dynamic specialist into the core roster (if ever allowed)
    

### 3.2 Core Agent Responsibilities

- **Senior Software Developer**
    
    - Implements features and fixes
        
    - Produces diffs/patches/PR-ready output
        
    - Requests specialists only when needed
        
- **QA Agent**
    
    - Defines acceptance tests
        
    - Validates outcomes and reports failures
        
    - Acts as a quality gate: “pass or fail” with evidence
        

### 3.3 Dynamic Specialist Responsibilities

Dynamic specialists are:

- Single-purpose
    
- Time-bounded
    
- Output-bounded (must produce a defined artifact)
    
- Disposable by default (session-only unless promoted)
    

---

## 4) Spawning Policy

### 4.1 Core Principle

Agents may spawn other agents only to fill a **specific capability gap** that is blocking progress.

### 4.2 When to Spawn a Unique Agent

Spawn a specialist when:

- The task requires expertise not covered by the current roster
    
- The work benefits from a dedicated lens (security review, data modeling, UX writing, performance profiling, compliance checklist)
    
- The specialist can produce a bounded deliverable quickly
    

Do **not** spawn when:

- The work can be done by an existing agent with a clearer brief
    
- The spawn is used to avoid making a decision
    
- The scope is unclear
    

### 4.3 Spawn Depth Rules

Choose one operating mode:

**Mode A: Dev Lead Only (Tight Control)**

- Spawn depth = 1
    
- Only Dev Lead spawns any agents
    
- Core agents request spawns via a “Spawn Request” format
    

**Mode B: Polymath Delegation (Controlled Autonomy)**

- Spawn depth = 2
    
- Dev Lead can spawn core agents and specialists
    
- Core agents (Senior Dev/QA) can spawn **only** depth-2 specialists within an allowlist and scope limits
    
- Depth-2 specialists may not spawn further agents
    

---

## 5) Mandatory Spawn Specification

Every spawn must include a structured spec. No spec, no spawn.

### 5.1 Spawn Spec Template

## 5) Mandatory Spawn Specification

Every spawn must include a structured spec. No spec, no spawn.

### 5.1 Spawn Spec Template

**Role Name:**  
**Mission (1 sentence):**  
**Why this agent is needed (capability gap):**  
**Inputs Provided (files/links/context):**  
**Allowed Tools/Scopes:**  
**Forbidden Actions:**  
**Deliverables (explicit artifacts):**  
**Success Criteria:**  
**Stop Conditions:**  
**Time/Iteration Budget:**  
**Reporting Format:** (bullet summary + links/paths to artifacts)

### 5.2 Standard Deliverable Types

Examples:

- Security: threat model + top risks + mitigations + “must fix” list
    
- Data: schema proposal + migration strategy + edge cases
    
- UX: user flows + copy + error states
    
- Performance: bottleneck report + profiling evidence + recommended fixes
    
- QA: test plan + acceptance criteria + regression list
    

---

## 6) Tool and Permission Scoping

### 6.1 Least Privilege

Each agent receives only the tools and access required for its mission.

### 6.2 Scope Guardrails

- Restrict filesystem paths
    
- Restrict network calls
    
- Restrict secrets handling (secrets-as-handles only)
    
- Restrict destructive operations (must be Dev Lead approved)
    

### 6.3 Output Boundary

All agent outputs must be:

- Clearly attributable to an agent/run
    
- Structured and auditable
    
- Suitable for Dev Lead review and integration
    

---

## 7) Execution Workflow

### 7.1 Standard Flow (Dev Lead Managed)

1. Dev Lead breaks work into tasks and assigns to Senior Dev and QA.
    
2. Senior Dev implements; QA defines and runs verification.
    
3. If blocked by capability gap, a Spawn Request is created.
    
4. Dev Lead (or authorized agent under Mode B) spawns a specialist with a strict spec.
    
5. Specialist returns deliverables; Dev Lead integrates.
    
6. QA validates; Dev Lead declares done.
    

### 7.2 Continuous Verification Rule

No task is considered complete without:

- Evidence (tests/logs/checklist outcomes)
    
- QA confirmation (pass or explicit exception approved by Dev Lead)
    

---

## 8) Lifecycle Rules for Dynamic Specialists

### 8.1 Default: Ephemeral

Dynamic specialists are session-only and expire after:

- Deliverables are produced and integrated, or
    
- The stop condition triggers, or
    
- The time budget is exhausted
    

### 8.2 Promotion (Rare)

A dynamic specialist may be proposed for promotion into the core roster only if:

- It is repeatedly needed across multiple projects
    
- Its outputs are consistently high quality
    
- The Dev Lead approves the role definition and scope
    

---

## 9) Safety, Ethics, and Quality Gates

### 9.1 Hard Stops

Any agent must refuse and escalate to Dev Lead if asked to:

- Bypass safeguards, access unauthorized data, engage in wrongdoing
    
- Produce harmful, deceptive, exploitative, or privacy-invasive plans
    

### 9.2 Quality Gate

QA is the enforcement layer. If QA fails the work:

- Dev Lead must route fixes back to Senior Dev (or spawn a narrow specialist)
    
- The loop continues until pass or Dev Lead explicitly cancels the objective
    

---

## 10) Operator Controls

The human operator (you) can always:

- Halt a run
    
- Veto an approach
    
- Require a stricter scope
    
- Demand an audit-style report before continuing
    

---

## 11) Quick Start Configuration Guidance

- If you want maximum safety and predictability: **Mode A (spawn depth 1)**
    
- If you want polymath-like agility without runaway recursion: **Mode B (spawn depth 2 + allowlist + strict spawn specs)**

# Ai joose Factory - (Current State)

## Document Purpose

This is the official current state and system brief for the current **Ai joose Factory** implementation. It explains what the platform is, what it does, how it works, and how it is operated by a **1-2 human team**.

---
## 1) What Ai joose Factory Is

**Ai joose Factory** is a build-and-operate AI venture studio platform that delivers:

- AI Businesses-as-a-Service
- AI Applications
- AI Automations

The operating model is intentionally designed so **1-2 human operators** can run workflows that would normally require a larger team.

This implementation is unique because it treats agent delegation, memory, and trust as engineered system properties with verifiable guarantees. It enforces a supervisor–delegate architecture where Dottie controls when and how Polly/OpenClaw is invoked through a bounded tool interface with scoped inputs, outputs, and permissions, preventing “agent sprawl” and keeping authority centralized. It guarantees cross-surface continuity by making the gateway, not the UI, the source of session truth, so OpenWebUI and Telegram behave identically in multi-turn operation. It delivers durable memory as a reliability-first hybrid substrate with canonical truth, semantic recall, and graph context linked by stable IDs and synchronized via an outbox mechanism with recovery behavior, eliminating drift and partial writes. It provides auditable accountability through exported provenance headers and enforced truthful attribution, so the system can prove what tools and sources were actually used. Finally, it backs these guarantees with automated end-to-end validation, including UI-level checks, ensuring the uniqueness is not theoretical but continuously verifiable.

## 3) How It Works (Architecture)

### 3.1 Primary Runtime Path

1. User sends request from OpenWebUI, Telegram, or Discord.
2. Dottie runtime receives request.
3. For OpenWebUI/Telegram, gateway resolves stable session key and restores context.
4. Agent loop runs with cloud model primary and local model fallback.
5. Agent calls tools iteratively as needed.
6. Responses are returned to user with status-aware reasoning stream support in gateway.
7. Session turns are persisted for continuity.
8. Durable facts are saved to SQLite and propagated to Weaviate/Neo4j via outbox.

- Local fallback model: `gpt-oss:120b`

- Repeated tool-call loop prevention

### 3.3 Memory Retrieval Design

Two-stage retrieval is implemented:

1. Stage A candidate generation: SQLite FTS + recency, Weaviate semantic search, Neo4j graph-context links.
2. Stage B rerank/assemble: dedupe by `memory_id`, then score by semantic + keyword + recency + source quality + memory class + graph support.

Graph context is returned as support context with:

- Known links
- Possible links
- Source memory references

# OpenClaw Adapter for Agent Zero (Mac)

## Objective

Create an adapter tool that allows Mac (Agent Zero) to communicate with Polly (OpenClaw) the same way George would — giving her tasks, instructions, and receiving responses. This enables Mac to act as a senior teammate delegating work to Polly rather than using narrow pre-defined actions.

## Background

- **Polly** is OpenClaw's lead orchestrator/developer agent, already running at `http://ajf-openclaw:3000`
- **Mac** is Agent Zero, configured as a general AI assistant with delegation capabilities
- Currently no bridge exists between Agent Zero and OpenClaw's A2A protocol

## Scope

### What the Adapter Must Do

1. **Full A2A Communication** — Mac can send natural language messages to Polly and receive responses
2. **Task Delegation** — Mac can give Polly tasks, instructions, PRDs, and requirements
3. **Bidirectional** — Polly can respond with results, questions, or status updates
4. **Session Management** — Maintain context across multiple exchanges (Polly remembers ongoing work)
5. **Streaming Responses** — Real-time feedback from Polly as she works

### Allowed Actions (NOT narrow)

Instead of narrow actions like `openclaw_summarize`, the adapter exposes:

# Problem
List all available Neo4j databases on the server and verify the default database node/relationship counts.

# Solution
1. **Load configuration** – read the Neo4j connection details from `/Mac/data/usr/.env` (URI, username, password, and optionally the target database).
2. **Connect** – use the official `neo4j` Python driver.
3. **Show databases** – run the `SHOW DATABASES` Cypher command to list every database, its status, and which one is the default.
4. **Count nodes & relationships** in the *default* database (`neo4j`).
5. **(Optional) Count nodes & relationships** in the database specified by `NEO4J_DATABASE` (e.g., the GraphRAG DB) for a more complete view.
6. **Print a concise report** confirming the server is reachable.

```python
from neo4j import GraphDatabase
import os

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1️⃣ Load environment variables from .env (only the needed keys)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
env_path = '/Mac/data/usr/.env'
config = {
    "uri": None,
    "username": None,
    "password": None,
    "default_db": "neo4j",   # hard‑coded default
    "extra_db": None          # optional second DB (e.g., GraphRAG db)
}

with open(env_path) as f:
    for line in f:
        line = line.strip()
        if line.startswith('NEO4J_URI='):
            config["uri"] = line.split('=', 1)[1].strip().strip('"').strip("'")
        elif line.startswith('NEO4J_USERNAME='):
            config["username"] = line.split('=', 1)[1].strip().strip('"').strip("'")
        elif line.startswith('NEO4J_PASSWORD='):
            config["password"] = line.split('=', 1)[1].strip().strip('"').strip("'")
        elif line.startswith('NEO4J_DATABASE='):
            config["extra_db"] = line.split('=', 1)[1].strip().strip('"').strip("'")

# Safeguard – ensure required fields are present
if not all([config["uri"], config["username"], config["password"]]):
    raise RuntimeError('Missing required Neo4j connection info in .env')

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2️⃣ Initialise driver
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
driver = GraphDatabase.driver(
    config["uri"],
    auth=(config["username"], config["password"]))

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
# 3️⃣ Helper to count nodes & relationships for a given DB
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
def count_entities(session):
    node_cnt = session.run('MATCH (n) RETURN count(n) AS c').single()['c']
    rel_cnt = session.run('MATCH ()-[r]->() RETURN count(r) AS c').single()['c']
    return node_cnt, rel_cnt

with driver.session(database=config["default_db"]) as session:
    # -----------------------------------------------------------------
    # 4️⃣ List all databases
    # -----------------------------------------------------------------
    print('🗄️  Available Neo4j Databases:')
    for rec in session.run('SHOW DATABASES'):
        print(f"  {rec['name']:20} | Status: {rec['currentStatus']:15} | Default: {rec['default']}")

    # -----------------------------------------------------------------
    # 5️⃣ Node/relationship counts for the default DB
    # -----------------------------------------------------------------
    def_cnt, rel_cnt = count_entities(session)
    print(f"\n✅ Default database '{config['default_db']}' – Nodes: {def_cnt}, Relationships: {rel_cnt}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
# 6️⃣ (Optional) Statistics for the extra DB defined in .env
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
if config["extra_db"]:
    with driver.session(database=config["extra_db"]) as session:
        node_cnt, rel_cnt = count_entities(session)
        print(f"\n✅ Extra database '{config['extra_db']}' – Nodes: {node_cnt}, Relationships: {rel_cnt}")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
# 7️⃣ Clean‑up
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------
driver.close()
print('\n✅ Neo4j server is reachable and statistics reported')
```

Running this script prints a formatted list of all Neo4j databases on the server, then shows the node and relationship counts for the default `neo4j` database (and, if defined, for the `NEO4J_DATABASE` from the .env). This confirms the server is alive and the data graph is accessible.
